stem: Let \( f(x, y) = [[a]]x^{3}y + ([[b]])xy^{2} + ([[c]])x \). What is the first-order partial derivative \( f_{x}([[x0]], [[y0]]) \) (the partial derivative with respect to \( x \), evaluated at the point \( ([[x0]], [[y0]]) \))?
solution: Treating \( y \) as a constant, \( f_{x}(x, y) = 3([[a]])x^{2}y + ([[b]])y^{2} + ([[c]]) \). Evaluating at \( ([[x0]], [[y0]]) \) gives \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the function value \( f([[x0]], [[y0]]) \), not its partial derivative.
This is \( f_{y}([[x0]], [[y0]]) \), the partial derivative with respect to the other variable.
This drops the factor of 3 from differentiating \( x^{3} \).
This omits the constant term \( ([[c]]) \) whose \( x \)-derivative is \( ([[c]]) \).
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(x, y) = ([[a]]x + [[b]])(xy + [[c]]) \), a product of two factors that both depend on \( x \). What is the first-order partial derivative \( f_{x}([[x0]], [[y0]]) \), found using the product rule (with \( y \) held constant)?
solution: By the product rule (treating \( y \) as a constant), \( f_{x}(x,y) = [[a]](xy + [[c]]) + ([[a]]x + [[b]])y \). Evaluating at \( ([[x0]], [[y0]]) \) gives \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This keeps only the first product-rule term, dropping \( ([[a]]x+[[b]])y \) entirely.
This keeps only the second product-rule term, dropping \( [[a]](xy+[[c]]) \) entirely.
This is the function value \( f([[x0]],[[y0]]) \), not its partial derivative.
This multiplies the coefficients of the two factors instead of applying the product rule.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(x, y) = \dfrac{[[a]]x^{2} + [[b]]y}{x + [[c]]} \). What is the first-order partial derivative \( f_{x}([[x0]], [[y0]]) \), found using the quotient rule (with \( y \) held constant)?
solution: By the quotient rule (treating \( y \) as a constant), \( f_{x}(x,y) = \dfrac{2([[a]])x \cdot (x+[[c]]) - ([[a]]x^{2}+[[b]]y)}{(x+[[c]])^{2}} \). Evaluating at \( ([[x0]], [[y0]]) \) gives \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to square the denominator from the quotient rule.
This swaps the order of subtraction in the quotient-rule numerator (sign error) while still squaring the denominator.
This differentiates only the numerator and ignores the quotient rule's second term and squared denominator.
This is the function value \( f([[x0]],[[y0]]) \), not its partial derivative.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(x, y) = [[a]]([[b]]x + [[c]]y + [[d]])^{[[n]]} \). What is the first-order partial derivative \( f_{x}([[x0]], [[y0]]) \), found using the chain rule (with \( y \) held constant)?
solution: By the chain rule (treating \( y \) as a constant), \( f_{x}(x,y) = [[a]]\cdot [[n]]([[b]]x + [[c]]y + [[d]])^{[[n]]-1}\cdot [[b]] \). Evaluating at \( ([[x0]], [[y0]]) \) gives \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the factor of \( [[b]] \) from differentiating the inner linear expression.
This drops the outer coefficient \( [[a]] \).
This forgets to reduce the exponent by 1, using \( ([[n]]) \) instead of \( ([[n]]-1) \).
This is the function value \( f([[x0]],[[y0]]) \), not its partial derivative.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(x, y) = [[a]]e^{[[b]]x + [[c]]y + ([[d]])} \) (note the exponent is engineered to equal \( 0 \) at \( ([[x0]], [[y0]]) \), so \( f([[x0]],[[y0]])=[[a]] \)). What is the first-order partial derivative \( f_{x}([[x0]], [[y0]]) \), found using the chain rule (with \( y \) held constant)?
solution: By the chain rule (treating \( y \) as constant), \( f_{x}(x,y) = [[a]]([[b]])e^{[[b]]x+[[c]]y+([[d]])} \). At \( ([[x0]],[[y0]]) \) the exponent is \( 0 \), so \( e^{0}=1 \) and \( f_{x} = [[a]]([[b]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to multiply by the inner derivative \( [[b]] \), using the function value \( f=a \) instead.
This keeps only the inner-derivative factor \( [[b]] \) and drops the outer coefficient \( [[a]] \).
This multiplies \( [[a]] \) by the coefficient of \( y \) instead of the coefficient of \( x \).
This adds the two coefficients instead of multiplying them.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(x, y) = [[a]]\ln([[b]]x + [[c]]y + ([[d]])) \) (the argument of \( \ln \) is engineered to equal \( 1 \) at \( ([[x0]], [[y0]]) \)). What is the first-order partial derivative \( f_{x}([[x0]], [[y0]]) \), found using the chain rule (with \( y \) held constant)?
solution: By the chain rule (treating \( y \) as constant), \( f_{x}(x,y) = \dfrac{[[a]]([[b]])}{[[b]]x+[[c]]y+([[d]])} \). At \( ([[x0]],[[y0]]) \) the argument of \( \ln \) is \( 1 \), so \( f_{x} = [[a]]([[b]])/1 = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This inverts the multiplication, dividing \( a \) by \( b \) instead of multiplying them.
This uses the coefficient of \( y \) in the denominator instead of the coefficient of \( x \) in the numerator.
This drops the outer coefficient \( [[a]] \) entirely.
This flips the sign of the correct derivative.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(x, y) = [[a]]y^{2}\sin(x) + ([[b]])xy \). What is the first-order partial derivative \( f_{x}(0, [[y0]]) \) (the partial derivative with respect to \( x \), evaluated at the point where \( x=0 \), \( y=[[y0]] \)), found using the product/chain rule with \( \sin \) and \( \cos \)?
solution: By the product/chain rule (treating \( y \) as constant), \( f_{x}(x,y) = [[a]]y^{2}\cos(x) + ([[b]])y \). At \( x=0 \), \( \cos(0)=1 \), so \( f_{x}(0,[[y0]]) = [[a]]([[y0]])^{2} + ([[b]])([[y0]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the \( \cos(0)=1 \) term entirely, keeping only the linear part of the product rule.
This drops the linear term \( by \) from the product rule, keeping only the trig part.
This uses \( y_{0} \) instead of \( y_{0}^{2} \) in the trig-derived term, as if the exponent on \( y \) were forgotten.
This flips the sign of the trig-derived term.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.

stem: Let \( f(x, y) = [[a]]\sqrt{[[b]]x + [[c]]y + ([[d]])} \) (the radicand is engineered to equal \( [[k]]^{2} \) at \( ([[x0]], [[y0]]) \)). What is the first-order partial derivative \( f_{x}([[x0]], [[y0]]) \), found using the chain rule?
distractor_rationales: This forgets the factor of 2 that comes from differentiating a square root.
This squares the radical value in the denominator instead of leaving it as \( 2k \).
This multiplies by the radical value instead of dividing by twice it.
This drops the coefficient \( [[b]] \) of the inner linear expression.
This is off by a half from the correct value.
This is off by a half from the correct value.

stem: A firm's output is modeled by \( Q(L, K) = [[a]]L^{2}K + ([[b]])LK^{2} + ([[c]])L \), where \( L \) is labor and \( K \) is capital. The marginal product of labor is the partial derivative \( Q_{L} \). What is the marginal product of labor at \( (L, K) = ([[L0]], [[K0]]) \)?
solution: Treating \( K \) as a constant, \( Q_{L}(L, K) = 2([[a]])LK + ([[b]])K^{2} + ([[c]]) \). Evaluating at \( ([[L0]], [[K0]]) \) gives \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the output level \( Q([[L0]], [[K0]]) \), not its marginal (partial derivative) with respect to labor.
This is the marginal product of capital \( Q_{K} \), differentiating with respect to the wrong variable.
This drops the factor of 2 from differentiating \( L^{2} \).
This omits the constant term \( [[c]] \) whose \( L \)-derivative is \( [[c]] \).
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.
This is off by a small constant from the correct value.
